analysis.problem: High poverty rates persist in Sub-Saharan Africa and Latin America, with over 25% of the population in Latin America and the Caribbean projected to live in poverty in 2025
analysis.root_cause: Inequality and unequal distribution of wealth and resources, exacerbated by factors such as colonialism, corruption, and lack of access to education and job opportunities
leverage_points: Governments and policymakers in Sub-Saharan Africa and Latin America

proposed_actions.action: Implement progressive taxation and social welfare policies to reduce income inequality
proposed_actions.measurable_goal: Reduce the Gini coefficient by 10% within the next 5 years

proposed_actions.action: Invest in education and job training programs to increase access to opportunities
proposed_actions.measurable_goal: Increase the percentage of population with access to secondary education by 20% within the next 10 years

evidence: Sub-Saharan Africa produces over 70% of the world's cobalt, but many countries in the region struggle with high poverty rates
The poverty rate in Latin America and the Caribbean is projected to fall to 25.2 percent in 2025, but this is still a significant proportion of the population
analysis.generalization: This problem is connected to the domains of planet (resource distribution) and human (access to education and opportunities), and has implications for the long-term sustainability of civilizations
analysis.summary: High poverty rates in Sub-Saharan Africa and Latin America are a major concern, and addressing the root causes of inequality and unequal distribution of resources is crucial to reducing poverty and promoting sustainable development

raw_items.title: Sub-Saharan Africa - Wikipedia
raw_items.summary: Sub-Saharan Africa is a key player in the global minerals market, producing over 70% of the world's cobalt and hosting about 50% of its reserves in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC).

raw_items.title: Nine key facts about poverty and inequality in Latin America
raw_items.summary: July 16, 2024 - GDP in Latin America & the Caribbean will expand by 1.6% in 2024 and a growth of 2.7% and 2.6% are expected for 2025 and 2026. These rates are the lowest compared to all other regions in the world, and insufficient to drive prosperity.
